lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market producers that repeatedly manufacture, polymerize, mix, or blend resins and plastics materials on a custom, toll, or customer-specification basis for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Large integrated commodity-resin producers, businesses selling only noncustomized proprietary resins, custom compounders that only process purchased resin outside NAICS 325211, captive plants, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industrial customers buy recurring custom resin campaigns or volumes under formulation and performance specifications, with revenue based on conversion fees, per-pound pricing, or formulas that may adjust for monomer, feedstock, and energy costs.
lens.deviation_from_default: Census explicitly includes both custom resin manufacturing or blending and noncustomized synthetic-resin production in this code. The lens retains the recurring custom and toll subset and excludes pure commodity production so eligibility and commercial retention describe a coherent outsourced service.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all chemical manufacturing rather than eligible 325211 custom-resin firms, and ILO reports global GenAI task exposure by broad occupation. I assign higher exposure to formulation support, quoting, scheduling, purchasing, quality documentation, laboratory data review, regulatory work, and engineering analysis and lower exposure to reactor operation, maintenance, material handling, sampling, packaging, and physical laboratory tasks. Broad chemical data may overstate cognitive work in continuous commodity plants but understate specification and formulation work in the retained custom subset.
a.rationale: Custom resin producers combine physical polymerization and blending with substantial formulation, specification, batch-record, certificate, planning, and customer-support work. AI can reduce parts of the information workload, while existing process controls and physical plant tasks are not current unautomated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from at least ten eligible custom or toll resin plants, tagged for current automation and actual workflow time, would materially change this range.
caveats: BLS data cover NAICS 325 rather than this six-digit code or the custom-resin subset.
ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution and emphasizes generative AI.
Formulation-intensive batch plants and commodity polymer trains have materially different occupation mixes.

rho.rationale: Planning, quality, formulation support, regulatory, and knowledge workflows can be implemented within five years, but process-safety validation, customer change control, product-liability risk, cybersecurity, fragmented historian and laboratory data, and auditability constrain deployment. EPA's air rules and recycled-feedstock specification concerns reinforce the need for traceable, qualified outputs.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-plant pilots measuring accepted AI outputs, exception rates, validation effort, labor hours, and quality outcomes in formulation search, scheduling, batch review, maintenance, and regulatory workflows would materially change implementation.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation for lower-middle-market custom resin manufacturers.
Customer approvals may require revalidation even when AI changes only internal workflow.

e.rationale: Unlike many manufacturing codes, the Census definition explicitly includes manufacturing and mixing or blending resins on a custom basis as well as noncustomized synthetic resins. That establishes a real service-model population, although large commodity producers and purchased-resin compounders fall outside the retained lens.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the 359 dataset firms using custom or toll contract revenue, in-house polymerization, ownership of formulations and feedstock, recurring customer programs, captive status, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and may include commodity producers whose economics do not resemble the custom subset.
Custom compounding of purchased resin is classified in NAICS 325991 and should not be counted even when market descriptions blur the boundary.

s5.bridge: Census reports owner age among responding U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers in eligible 325211 firms. I use the 51% age-55-or-older share only as succession pressure, then reduce it for the five-year window, institutional ownership, internal succession, non-sale exits, environmental and product liabilities, buyer financing, and customer qualification risk. The cross-industry proxy may overstate transfers for capital-intensive resin plants but understate founder succession in niche custom formulators.
s5.rationale: The larger estimated firm population and explicit custom segment create plausible seller supply, but a qualifying sale requires transferable formulations, customer programs, permits, plant condition, technical staff, and defensible environmental and product-liability history.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year closed-deal data from plastics and specialty-chemical databases, state and EPA permit ownership changes, and interviews with resin-sector M&A advisers and eligible owners would materially change the probability.
caveats: Census owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
Age does not establish sale intent, a transferable business, or a completed control transaction.

q.rationale: Customer qualification, formulation know-how, performance consistency, and switching cost can protect benefits tied to yield, quality, development speed, and turnaround. Feedstock-indexed pricing, open-book tolling, procurement benchmarking, volume contracts, and rebids can pass visible labor or energy savings to customers.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract sample showing term, renewal cadence, monomer and energy indices, open-book provisions, yield guarantees, development fees, gainsharing, minimum volumes, and historical repricing after process improvements would materially change retention.
caveats: No public contract sample measures productivity pass-through for eligible custom resin producers.
Capture can be much lower for benchmarked commodity formulations and higher for qualified proprietary customer programs.

d5.bridge: EPA cites a global projection that plastics production may almost triple by 2060, and EIA projects broad bulk-chemical feedstock growth, but neither measures five-year constant-price U.S. demand for eligible custom 325211 processing. I translate continued physical plastics use into modest base growth and widen the range for packaging reduction, recycled-content shifts, regulation, imports, industrial cycles, substitution, and new integrated capacity. The global long-horizon projection may overstate near-term U.S. custom-resin demand, while broad virgin-feedstock models may understate circular and advanced-recycling opportunities.
d5.rationale: Resins remain essential inputs across packaging, construction, transportation, electronics, medical products, and durable goods. Demand faces environmental pressure and redesign, while advanced recycling and recycled-content requirements can create new formulation and qualification work; EPA notes both weak overall U.S. plastics recycling and technical constraints on recycled resin consistency.
d5.change_evidence: Customer program forecasts, product-level shipment and import data, packaging and vehicle build outlooks, virgin and recycled capacity announcements, contract backlog, and recurring-volume histories for eligible firms would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The global production projection is much broader and longer-dated than the screened U.S. service basket.
Environmental policy can reduce some virgin-resin demand while increasing custom recycled-content formulation work.
One ratio masks major differences among resin families and end markets.

o.rationale: Remaining demand requires physical polymerization or blending, formulation control, testing, safe handling, specification release, and responsibility for consistent resin performance. Software can reduce development and coordination labor but cannot deliver qualified material; displacement is primarily customer insourcing, supplier consolidation, material substitution, or elimination of a product use.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy studies, announced captive resin capacity, supplier-consolidation records, material-substitution roadmaps, and validated autonomous-plant evidence removing external operational accountability would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate does not protect current product uses from source reduction or substitution, which is reflected in d5.
Operator-required manufacturing can migrate to a larger integrated supplier even when material demand persists.

narrative.executive_view: Plastics material and resin manufacturing contains an explicitly recognized custom-production segment, making the outsourced-service lens more natural than in many chemical codes. The opportunity remains narrower than the full industry because large commodity producers and purchased-resin compounders are excluded, and plant economics remain more exposed to feedstock, utilization, and product mix than labor alone.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve formulation search, quoting, campaign scheduling, purchasing, laboratory and batch review, deviation handling, maintenance triage, regulatory documentation, and customer technical support. Physical reaction and blending, material handling, sampling, maintenance execution, and accountable release remain less substitutable.
narrative.value_capture: Qualification costs and formulation know-how can preserve value from better yield, quality, development speed, and turnaround. Transparent savings are vulnerable to monomer-index formulas, open-book tolling, procurement benchmarks, renewal bids, and customer concentration.
narrative.firm_availability: The explicit custom category and larger raw firm count support a plausible target population, but classification is essential because commodity resin producers and NAICS 325991 purchased-resin compounders do not qualify. Transfer diligence must cover formulations, customer approvals, environmental history, product liability, plant condition, and technical staff.
narrative.demand_durability: Physical resin demand should persist across broad end markets, but virgin-plastics growth is contested by source reduction, recycling, regulation, and substitution. Circularity can also create new work in recycled-feedstock qualification, stabilization, blending, traceability, and customer-specific performance.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The case weakens if diligence reveals commodity price-taking, little custom revenue, low addressable labor, open-book pass-through, obsolete capacity, or material environmental and product-liability exposure. Resin-family concentration, customer qualification cycles, volatile monomer spreads, and policy shifts can overwhelm AI benefits.
narrative.principal_driver: An explicit custom-manufacturing segment combines recurring qualified formulations with information-heavy workflows that AI can improve.
narrative.principal_weakness: Commodity exposure, feedstock economics, environmental pressure, and liability can dominate the modest labor opportunity.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 325211 Definition
sources.supports: Census defines 325211 to include manufacturing resins, plastics materials, and thermoplastic elastomers and mixing or blending resins on a custom basis, as well as noncustomized synthetic resins; it assigns custom compounding of purchased resin to 325991.

sources.title: Chemical Manufacturing: NAICS 325
sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 chemical-manufacturing employment of 124,330 chemical equipment operators, 17,600 chemical technicians, 27,530 chemists, 25,810 mixing and blending operators, and 57,400 packaging and filling operators.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: Policies to Manage the Transition
sources.supports: ILO reports 24% of clerical tasks at high automation exposure and 58% at medium exposure, while other occupational groups have 1% to 4% highly exposed and no more than 25% medium-exposed tasks.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey for data year 2018.

sources.title: Chemical Manufacturing Area Sources: National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants
sources.supports: EPA lists plastic materials and resins manufacturing among nine regulated chemical area-source categories and states that amendments were finalized on March 28, 2026.

sources.title: Advanced Recycling of Plastics
sources.supports: EPA states that global plastics production is projected to almost triple by 2060, describes limitations from variable recycled-plastic properties, and says resulting oils and monomers must meet qualified product specifications for new resin manufacture.

sources.title: Annual Energy Outlook 2026 Narrative
sources.supports: EIA projects industrial liquid-fuel use to grow across all cases, with HGL and naphtha feedstocks in bulk chemicals helping drive growth; industrial petroleum use rises from 5.5 million barrels per day in 2025 to 6.3 million to 7.7 million in 2050 in most cases.

sources.title: Plastics: Material-Specific Data
sources.supports: EPA reports that three million tons of plastics were recycled in 2018, an overall recycling rate of 8.7%, and describes resin use across containers and packaging.
